audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel / Shemuel Bet chapter 4, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Ish-bosheth's collapse after Abner's death, Beerothite identity, Mephibosheth's childhood injury, domestic assassination, beheading, providential claims, David's precedent from Ziklag, summary execution, mutilation, public display, and burial.

verses.restored: Son of Saul heard that Abner had died in Hebron, and his hands became weak, and all Israel was dismayed.

Son of Saul had two men, commanders of raiding bands. Name of one was Baanah, and name of second Rechab, sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, from children of Benjamin, because Beeroth also was counted to Benjamin.
Beerothites had fled to Gittaim and have been resident aliens there until this day.

Jonathan son of Saul had a son injured in his feet. He was five years old when report concerning Saul and Jonathan came from Jezreel. His nurse carried him and fled, and in her hurrying to flee he fell and became disabled. His name was Mephibosheth.
Sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, Rechab and Baanah, went and came at heat of day to house of Ish-bosheth while he was lying down at noon.
They came into midst of house as if taking wheat, and they struck him in abdomen. Rechab and Baanah his brother escaped.
They entered house while he was lying upon his bed in his sleeping room. They struck him and put him to death, removed his head, took his head, and walked by way of Arabah all night.

They brought head of Ish-bosheth to David at Hebron and said to king, Look, head of Ish-bosheth son of Saul your enemy, who sought your life. YHWH has given to my lord the king vengeance this day upon Saul and upon his offspring.
David answered Rechab and Baanah his brother, sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, and said to them, As YHWH lives, who redeemed my life from all distress:
when one told me, saying, Look, Saul is dead, and he was in his own eyes as one bringing good news, I seized him and killed him in Ziklag—this was the reward I gave him for his news.
How much more when wicked men have killed a righteous man in his house upon his bed. Now shall I not seek his blood from your hand and remove you from earth?
David commanded young men, and they killed them, cut off their hands and their feet, and hung them beside pool in Hebron. They took head of Ish-bosheth and buried it in grave of Abner in Hebron.

translation_notes: Ish-bosheth's weakness after Abner's death reflects political dependency and fear, not deficient masculinity.
Beerothite and Benjaminite identities locate the assassins but do not explain their violence or create inherited guilt.
Mephibosheth's injury occurs during a caregiver's urgent flight from war. Disability is not Divine punishment, dynastic contamination, or proof of a ruined life.
The nurse acts to save a child amid state collapse. Her hurried movement should not be reduced to individual blame for his lifelong impairment.
Verses 6-7 contain repetition and textual difficulty concerning entry and killing. The source seam should remain visible.
Rechab and Baanah invoke YHWH to interpret assassination as providential vengeance. Religious framing does not make murder righteous.

David rejects the killing yet again orders immediate execution without recorded trial. He then mutilates and publicly displays the bodies; refusal of regicide does not make his punishment ethically clean.
Cutting hands and feet targets the body parts associated with the crime and escape, but retaliatory mutilation remains state terror.
Ish-bosheth's head is finally buried with Abner. Restoration of burial dignity follows multiple acts of corpse desecration.
safety_note: Maximum safeguards: Do not shame disability, blame the nurse, stigmatize Beerothites or Benjaminites, or treat assassination as providence. Do not glorify David's execution, mutilation, or public display as ideal justice. Center war-displaced children, domestic vulnerability, due process, and dignity of bodies.
